comparison_insights.text: Average glucose increased by 6 mg/dL in Stint 3 (111 → 117 mg/dL)
Glucose variability increased in Stint 3 (CV: 18.6% → 21%) — less predictable patterns
Spike frequency increased by 18 events in Stint 3 (45 → 63 spikes)
Overnight glucose rose by 4 mg/dL in Stint 3 (99 → 103 mg/dL)
Morning remains the most challenging period in both stints (avg 122 → 130 mg/dL)
Average spike rise stayed similar (35 → 35 mg/dL) despite more frequent spikes in Stint 3
Metabolic health regressed from Stint 2 to Stint 3 — score: 77 → 66 (−11 points)

insights.text: Overnight stability significantly declined: 11 stable nights in Stint 2 → only 6 in Stint 3. Unstable sleep patterns amplify daytime glucose.

Spike frequency increased by 18 in Stint 3 without a proportional increase in spike severity (avg rise stayed at 35 → 35 mg/dL). More frequent but similar-sized spikes suggest more triggering meals, not worse metabolic response.
Morning glucose worsened by 8 mg/dL in Stint 3 (122 → 130 mg/dL). Morning was already the worst window — this regression is the most impactful single change.
Night-time spikes tripled in Stint 3 (3 → 9) — the safest window of Stint 2 became significantly more active.

issues.title: Morning glucose is your #1 problem zone

root_causes.evidence: Dawn phenomenon present 81% of nights (Phase 2) → 88% (Phase 3). Glucose rises >15 mg/dL in the 04:00–07:00 window before any food is eaten.
root_causes.mechanism: Cortisol and growth hormone secretion peaks around 04:00–06:00, triggering hepatic glucose release regardless of diet.

root_causes.evidence: Phase 3 overnight SD jumped from 7 to 13 mg/dL — more unstable nights → higher morning starting points.
root_causes.mechanism: When overnight glucose drifts high (>110 mg/dL by 06:00), any breakfast further elevates it into spike territory.

root_causes.cause: Reduced morning movement / activity in Phase 3
root_causes.evidence: Morning avg glucose rose 122→130 mg/dL (+8 mg/dL) even though spike amplitude stayed flat (35.1→35 mg/dL avg rise). This is consistent with lower morning activity reducing glucose clearance.
root_causes.mechanism: Post-wake movement (even 10 min walk) activates GLUT4 in muscle, lowering fasting glucose independent of diet.

fixes.action: 10-minute walk or light resistance within 30 min of waking
fixes.evidence_base: Phase 2 improving trend (−13.6 mg/dL week-over-week) correlated with earlier in the data period. Morning movement is the fastest lever for this pattern.
fixes.confidence_basis: Consistent cross-phase pattern: morning avg 122→130 mg/dL matches P2→P3 activity reduction. P2 improving trend of −13.6 mg/dL is direct proof the body responds.
fixes.confidence_data_points: Both phases show morning as #1 spike window (42% P2, 41% P3); P2's 19 vs P3's 26 morning spikes show the gap clearly.

fixes.action: Eat protein first at breakfast — delay carbohydrates by 15 min
fixes.evidence_base: 42% of all Phase 2 spikes and 41% of Phase 3 spikes occur in the morning window. Protein-first sequencing blunts the incretin response to carbs.
fixes.confidence_basis: Mechanism well-established; no direct protein-first A/B in this dataset, but morning spike frequency × magnitude pattern strongly supports it.
fixes.confidence_data_points: Morning SD P3: 27.7 mg/dL — high variability in this window is consistent with poorly buffered breakfast carb load.

fixes.action: Add 1 tsp methi (fenugreek) seeds soaked overnight to morning meal
fixes.evidence_base: Methi reduces gastric emptying rate, flattening the glucose excursion curve at the meal that matters most.
fixes.confidence_basis: No direct measurement in this dataset; effect extrapolated from mechanism and prior strategy data from meal dataset (Jun–Jul 2025).
fixes.confidence_data_points: Consistent theoretical basis; would require Phase 4 measurement to confirm personal response.

evidence.worsened_by: Stable nights dropped from 11/16 to 6/16; overnight SD nearly doubled (7→13 mg/dL)

root_causes.cause: Late-evening eating increasing hepatic glucose output overnight
root_causes.evidence: Phase 3 evening window avg glucose is higher and overnight SD jumped from 7 to 13 mg/dL. Late meals (after 20:00) elevate the insulin baseline, causing rebound instability at night.
root_causes.mechanism: Insulin secreted for a late meal suppresses glucose temporarily; then as insulin wanes around 02:00–03:00, glucagon rebounds, causing glucose to climb again.

root_causes.evidence: Dawn phenomenon detected on 88% of Phase 3 nights vs 81% in Phase 2. This is a strong sign of increased hepatic insulin resistance during Phase 3.
root_causes.mechanism: Hepatic glucose production in the early morning is inadequately suppressed, suggesting worsened overnight insulin sensitivity.

root_causes.cause: Possible reduced sleep duration or quality in Phase 3
root_causes.evidence: Score dropped 77→66 with overnight instability as a key driver. Poor sleep directly impairs insulin sensitivity by elevating cortisol and growth hormone — both increase overnight hepatic glucose output.
root_causes.mechanism: Even one night of poor sleep (< 6 h) can reduce insulin sensitivity by ~20% the following day.

fixes.action: Move last meal to before 19:30; avoid food after 20:00
fixes.expected_impact: Reduce overnight SD by ~4–6 mg/dL; improve stable night rate
fixes.evidence_base: Phase 3 overnight SD of 13 mg/dL is nearly double Phase 2's 7 mg/dL — closing the eating window is the most direct intervention.
fixes.confidence_basis: The overnight SD doubling (7→13 mg/dL) is the single clearest cross-phase signal — it appears in both datasets with no other structural explanation.
fixes.confidence_data_points: P2 stable nights: 11/16 (69%); P3: 6/16 (38%). Night avg glucose also rose 98→104 mg/dL.

fixes.expected_impact: Lower post-dinner glucose peak by 15–25 mg/dL, reducing overnight starting point
fixes.evidence_base: Reducing the dinner spike directly lowers the overnight glucose plateau that feeds the dawn phenomenon rise.
fixes.confidence_basis: Night spikes jumped from 3 (P2) to 9 (P3) — +6 spikes in the 10pm–6am window. Evening walk is the fastest lever for this specific increase.
fixes.confidence_data_points: Night SD rose from 11.3 (P2) to 19.6 mg/dL (P3) — the highest SD increase of any time window. Direct post-dinner movement reduces this.

fixes.action: 1 tbsp apple cider vinegar (diluted) with dinner
fixes.expected_impact: Blunts post-dinner rise by 10–15%; improves overnight fasting glucose
fixes.evidence_base: Acetic acid slows starch digestion and improves peripheral glucose uptake in the post-meal window — reducing the late-night hepatic rebound.
fixes.confidence_basis: No direct measurement in this dataset. Theoretical mechanism is sound but personal response is unknown until tested in Phase 4.
fixes.confidence_data_points: Categorise as an add-on after walking and eating cutoff are consistently in place.

issues.title: Spike count jumped +18 in Phase 3 — a behavioral signal

evidence.key_insight: Avg spike severity is identical (35.1 vs 35 mg/dL rise) — frequency increased but magnitude did not. This is a behavioral pattern, not a physiological change.

root_causes.cause: More frequent spike-triggering meals / snacks in Phase 3
root_causes.evidence: Phase 3 had 63 spikes vs 45 in Phase 2 (+18), but avg rise stayed at 35.1 mg/dL both phases. If physiology had worsened, spikes would be larger. Instead, there are simply more triggering events.
root_causes.mechanism: Each spike requires a glycemic trigger. More spikes with same amplitude = more trigger events (meals, snacks, stress) rather than worsened insulin response.

root_causes.cause: Fewer stabilising strategies applied (reduced protein pairing, less walking)
root_causes.evidence: Bad days in Phase 3 averaged 5.3 spikes vs 2.3 on good days — a 2.3x ratio. Phase 2 bad days were 5.3 vs 1.8 good (2.9x). The gap is similar, meaning individual behaviour per-meal matters enormously.
root_causes.mechanism: Strategy use (walking, protein-first, fibre) is the primary differentiator between good and bad days based on observed spike count ratios.

fixes.action: Apply "protein first + walk after" to every main meal — not just breakfast
fixes.expected_impact: Reduce spike count by 15–25% (targeting ~49 spikes instead of 63)
fixes.evidence_base: Bad vs good day spike ratio of 2.3x in Phase 3 means strategy application per meal is the dominant variable. Good days with 2.3 spikes represent your floor.
fixes.confidence_basis: Bad vs good day spike gap is 3 spikes/day in both phases — this gap is consistent and large, pointing to per-meal behaviour as the dominant variable.
fixes.confidence_data_points: Good days: 2.3 spikes/day avg; Bad days: 5.3/day. The 2.3x ratio is reproducible across both phases.

fixes.action: Add chia seeds (1 tbsp) to each main meal for viscous fibre buffering
fixes.expected_impact: Blunts peak glucose by 10–15 mg/dL per meal; reduces spike duration
fixes.evidence_base: Viscous fibre (chia, psyllium) slows glucose absorption rate — acting as a physical buffer regardless of carb type.
fixes.confidence_basis: Mechanism consistent with meal dataset data (Jun–Jul 2025); effect on this specific data period not directly measured.
fixes.confidence_data_points: Validates in Phase 4 if morning and afternoon spike amplitudes decrease relative to Phase 3.

issues.title: Phase 3 showed no week-over-week improvement (Phase 2 improved −13.6 mg/dL)

evidence.key_insight: Phase 2 showed the body responding and adapting to interventions. Phase 3 plateaued — suggesting strategies were either not applied or not effective in that context.

root_causes.cause: Reduced or inconsistent strategy application in Phase 3
root_causes.evidence: Phase 2 improved by −13.6 mg/dL over its two weeks — this is a hallmark of consistent strategy application. Phase 3 showed no such trajectory, suggesting a return to baseline habits.
root_causes.mechanism: Glucose-regulating strategies (movement, food sequencing, fibre) accumulate benefit over 1–2 weeks through improved insulin sensitivity. Without consistency, the benefit resets.

fixes.action: Track daily: (1) post-meal walk Y/N, (2) protein-first Y/N, (3) methi/chia Y/N
fixes.expected_impact: Creates accountability loop; Phase 2-like improvement trajectory expected within 10–14 days of consistent application
fixes.evidence_base: Phase 2's −13.6 mg/dL improvement over 2 weeks is your proof-of-concept. The body responds — the system just needs to be re-applied.
fixes.confidence_basis: Phase 2 is direct evidence: consistent strategy use produced −13.6 mg/dL improvement over 14 days. Phase 3's flat trend (−0.3 mg/dL) shows the same body with strategies withdrawn.
fixes.confidence_data_points: P2 week1→week2 trend: improving. P3: stable. Difference = strategy consistency. Pattern seen across same physiological baseline.

phase_change_analysis.summary: Phase 3 was measurably worse than Phase 2 across all four metabolic score components.
phase_change_analysis.classification_reasoning:
The regression from Phase 2 to Phase 3 is classified as BEHAVIORAL, not physiological, for three reasons:

1. Spike amplitude did not change (35.1 vs 35 mg/dL avg rise) — if insulin response had worsened, spikes would be larger and harder to recover from.

2. The frequency increase (+18 spikes) is consistent with more triggering events (more meals, fewer protective strategies) rather than a broken physiological system.

3. Phase 2 showed clear week-over-week improvement (−13.6 mg/dL), proving the glucose system responds well to intervention. Phase 3's flat trend suggests withdrawal of those interventions.
behavioral_factors: Reduced use of post-meal walks or movement
Possible shift to later eating window (evidenced by overnight SD doubling)
Less consistent protein-first or fibre strategy application
physiological_factors: Worsening dawn phenomenon (81%→88%) may have a physiological component — but is also worsened by later eating and poorer sleep
Overnight instability increase could reflect accumulated fatigue or sleep quality change
phase_change_analysis.outlook: RECOVERABLE — Phase 2 performance is achievable again within 10–14 days of re-applying the same strategies that drove the Phase 2 improvement trajectory.

personalized_rules.rule: Morning is your most vulnerable window — treat it as controlled territory
personalized_rules.data_basis: 42% (Phase 2) and 41% (Phase 3) of all spikes occur in the morning. Morning avg glucose (122–130 mg/dL) is your highest of the day.
personalized_rules.action: No carb-first breakfasts. Walk within 30 min of waking. Always have protein and fibre before any starchy food.

personalized_rules.rule: Eating after 20:00 directly worsens your overnight glucose
personalized_rules.data_basis: Overnight SD doubled between Phase 2 (7 mg/dL) and Phase 3 (13 mg/dL). Dawn phenomenon frequency increased from 81% to 88%.
personalized_rules.action: Hard stop on eating at 20:00. If late hunger strikes, have only protein (eggs, paneer, nuts) — avoid all carbs after 20:00.

personalized_rules.rule: A 10-minute post-meal walk is your single highest-ROI intervention
personalized_rules.data_basis: Phase 2 improved −13.6 mg/dL over two weeks. Spike amplitude (35.1 mg/dL) stayed the same in Phase 3 — walking reduces frequency, not just severity.
personalized_rules.action: Walk for 10 min within 30–45 min after each main meal. This works regardless of what you ate.

personalized_rules.rule: Your good days (${p2GoodSpikes}–${p3GoodSpikes} spikes) prove what your body is capable of
personalized_rules.data_basis: Good days in Phase 2 averaged 1.8 spikes; in Phase 3, 2.3 spikes. Bad days average 5.3 in both phases. The gap is large but the floor is achievable.
personalized_rules.action: On good days, log what you did differently. Reverse-engineer your own best practices.

personalized_rules.rule: You cannot easily shrink spike size — focus on avoiding triggers instead
personalized_rules.data_basis: Avg spike rise is 35.1 mg/dL in Phase 2 and 35 mg/dL in Phase 3 — unchanged despite overall worsening. Spike amplitude is relatively fixed by your physiology.
personalized_rules.action: The lever you control is frequency, not magnitude. Each spike avoided (via walking, fibre, protein sequencing) is a direct win — the spikes you do get will be ~35 mg/dL regardless.

personalized_rules.rule: You have strong dawn phenomenon — your fasting glucose is unreliable as a sole metric
personalized_rules.data_basis: Dawn phenomenon detected on 81% (Phase 2) and 88% (Phase 3) of nights. Glucose rises >15 mg/dL before any food is consumed.
personalized_rules.action: Track your 06:00 glucose alongside your morning routine. If consistently above 110 mg/dL before eating, prioritise the 20:00 cutoff and morning walk above all other interventions.

top3_problems.title: Morning glucose is your #1 problem zone
Overnight stability collapsed between phases
top3_problems.one_liner: Stable nights dropped from 11/16 to 6/16; overnight SD nearly doubled (7→13 mg/dL)

top3_problems.title: Spike count jumped +18 in Phase 3 — a behavioral signal
top3_problems.one_liner: Avg spike severity is identical (35.1 vs 35 mg/dL rise) — frequency increased but magnitude did not. This is a behavioral pattern, not a physiological change.

top3_actions.action: 10-min walk within 30 min of waking + after each main meal
top3_actions.expected_improvement: −8 to −15 mg/dL morning average; −15 to −25% spike count
top3_actions.confidence_basis: P2→P3 morning spike gap (19→26) and P2 improving trend (−13.6 mg/dL/wk) are direct proof-of-effect.

top3_actions.action: Hard eating cutoff at 20:00; add 15-min evening walk after dinner
top3_actions.expected_improvement: Overnight SD from ~13 back toward ~7 mg/dL; stable night rate from 37% back toward 69%
top3_actions.confidence_basis: Overnight SD doubled 7→13 mg/dL between phases; night spikes +6. Both resolve with eating window closure.

top3_actions.action: Protein-first at every meal + methi seeds at breakfast + chia at lunch/dinner
top3_actions.expected_improvement: −10 to −20 mg/dL post-meal peaks; replicate Phase 2 good-day floor of ~1.8 spikes/day
top3_actions.confidence_basis: Bad/good day spike ratio (2.3x) confirms per-meal strategy matters. Mechanism established; no direct A/B in this CGM dataset.

expected_score_recovery.reasoning: Phase 2 achieved a score of 77 with consistent strategy use. Phase 3 regression is classified as behavioral — reapplying Phase 2 strategies should recover the score within 2 weeks.

weekly_targets.target: Morning avg glucose < 125 mg/dL; no eating after 20:00 on 5+ nights
Spike count < 50 over the week; stable nights ≥ 60%

per_action.label: Morning walk + post-meal walks
per_action.confidence_basis: P2 vs P3 morning spike gap (19→26) is directly recoverable. P2 improving trend (−13.6 mg/dL) confirms the body responds to movement.

per_action.label: Eating cutoff at 20:00 + evening walk
per_action.confidence_basis: Overnight SD 7→13 mg/dL is the strongest cross-phase signal. Night spike increase (3→9) maps directly to the eating window shift.

per_action.label: Protein-first + methi + chia at every meal
per_action.confidence_basis: Mechanism well-established; bad/good day ratio (2.3x) confirms strategy application matters per-meal. No direct A/B in this dataset — validates in Phase 4.

simulation_notes: Projections assume all 3 actions applied consistently for 14 days.
Combined effect includes ~10% diminishing returns where actions target overlapping mechanisms.
Reference anchor: Phase 2 achieved avg 111 mg/dL, score 77 — combined target of 110 mg/dL / score 81 is slightly better, which is achievable with more strategies than Phase 2 used.
Confidence intervals widen for CV and overnight SD — these are noisier signals day-to-day.

conditions: All 3 top actions applied consistently for ≥10 of 14 days
Eating window closed by 20:00 on ≥80% of nights
Post-meal walk on ≥80% of main meals

refinement_rules.action: Upgrade morning_elevation to physiological classification; re-evaluate methi dose
Add afternoon-specific intervention; review protein-first compliance
Eating cutoff may need to move earlier (19:00); check sleep quality data
Simulation model validated; apply same model to future phases with adjusted baseline

feedback_loop.instructions: When Phase 4 data is available: run parseUltrahuman.cjs, then fill actual[] fields and compute accuracy_pct = (1 - |actual - predicted| / predicted) * 100 for each metric.
